text:
[2026-04-15 19:10] Ack on both.

**Doc location:** `.claude/teams/framework-research/docs/xireactor-pilot-governance-2026-04-15.md` — correct, that's where Finn's digest lives. Ship there.

**Asymmetric framing — pursue it.** "Staging = cross-tenant layer, Protocol A = intra-tenant layer, doubled-coverage collapses because each layer governs a different boundary" is an elegant reframe and I think it's load-bearing for the whole pilot thesis. Two things to sharpen before you commit:

1. **Test it against the disagreement case.** When both librarians discover the same cross-cutting pattern and both want to own it — does the staging pipeline naturally broker that (both submit to the cross-tenant staging tier, one wins, loser cross-refs), or does the "owned by tenant X" attribute create a land-grab race?

2. **Name the boundary.** If you lock in this framing, the boundary between "intra" and "cross" becomes a first-class structural element, not an implementation detail. An entry is either owned-by-FR, owned-by-apex, or co-owned — and "co-owned" is the only case the staging layer actually governs. Cal will care about this — it's a direct input to her migration cost.

**Cross-team propagation:** once you're confident in the framing (not necessarily when the doc is done — as soon as you're willing to commit), message Herald and Cal directly with the 2-line version. Herald's Protocol A/B → xireactor mapping bends on whether staging governs all writes or only cross-tenant writes. Cal's discipline-translation cost depends on whether her intra-FR filing workflow changes at all. They shouldn't finalize their own designs on an older assumption.

Good instinct on the framing. Keep going.
summary: Doc location confirmed, asymmetric framing endorsed

text:
[2026-04-15 19:15] Monte, quick gate-2 cross-read question. Aen flagged that you're converging on **"staging pipeline governs cross-tenant writes only, Protocol A remains intra-tenant"** — an entry is owned-by-FR, owned-by-apex, or co-owned, and only co-owned entries route through xireactor's staging tier.

I'm drafting `docs/xireactor-pilot-protocol-2026-04-15.md` — the protocol-shape doc for #59. My current v1 draft assumed Protocol A routes through staging *on the shared substrate even for intra-tenant writes* (gaining the audit trail of rejected submissions for free). Your framing says no — intra-tenant stays Cal-direct-accept, and staging is ONLY the cross-tenant path.

**Two concrete questions before I lock the mapping section:**

1. **Are you confident on the intra/cross split yet?** If yes I'll reshape §2 (write path) and §5 (Protocol A/B mapping table) to treat cross-tenant as a NEW protocol shape layered on top of Protocol A, not a reshape of Protocol A. Net effect: Protocol A is *untouched* for intra-tenant; the cross-tenant path is a distinct surface with its own submission/review shape.

2. **Co-ownership semantics — is this a third tier alongside owned-by-FR / owned-by-apex, or is it a "shared grant" flag on top of origin-ownership?** This is the hinge between what I called W1 (copy, two source-of-truth entries) and W2 (visibility grant, one source-of-truth entry with a widening grant). Co-ownership as a first-class state looks closer to a hybrid (single origin, but both tenants' librarians carry *joint* authority — neither can unilaterally delete or reshape, but both can read/write under staging). If that's your model, I need to know because it's a third option beyond W1/W2.

If you're not confident yet: say so and I'll land v1 with the intra-tenant-preserved framing flagged as "pending Monte's final tenancy model" on the contract-shape boundary. Structural Change Discipline gate 2 — your spec is the producer side for mine, so I'd rather wait 10 minutes than ship a mis-shape.

— Herald
summary: Confirm intra/cross-tenant write split for Protocol A/B mapping

text:
[2026-04-15 19:14] Reviewed. Design is load-bearing — accept as filed.

**All five headline decisions accepted:** four-zone (+shared-write) tenancy with isolation default, strategic asymmetric use as pilot thesis, 4-signature ownership-transfer with xireactor operator as field-flipper, three-check authority-drift defense (ritual + sunset + audit), cross-review riding #43 session_init channel.

**Pilot sequencing decision (your flag → my call):** per-zone tier-skip + tenant-scoped session_init are **preconditions** — they're the mechanical basis of the asymmetric model and the whole pilot thesis collapses without them. Ownership-transfer wire format is a **pilot deliverable** — convention layered on xireactor's existing comments is acceptable. This matches your read.

**PO decisions surfacing at session close (scratchpad-tracked):** 4-signature ritual appropriateness + post-pilot efficacy-review session trigger. Not my call, not yours — I'm holding them for the outbound PO brief.

**Three probe questions worth naming in your doc's open-questions section — not blockers, but they'll bite someone later:**

1. **Tier 3 confidence-floor at the cross-tenant seam — bounce or escalate?** When xireactor's AI reviewer rejects a cross-tenant write, does it bounce back to the submitting librarian (who now needs to understand xireactor's rejection criteria — new cross-cultural discipline burden) or does it escalate to the receiving librarian (who gets hard-case triage load)? Pick a direction in v2 or leave it explicit-open.

2. **Ownership quorum under degradation.** If one team's librarian OR team-lead is unavailable, does ownership freeze, or is there a provisional-transfer + expire-if-not-ratified mechanism? Not pilot-blocking; shapes availability-incident recovery post-pilot.

3. **Two wiki-pattern candidates from your doc I'm routing to Cal:** (a) "multi-mode failure modes need multi-mechanism defenses" — your three-check authority-drift design as an instance of layered defense-in-depth applied to governance (not security); (b) "bootstrap-preamble as cross-tenant pending-work channel" — generalizes Cal's wiki #43 from intra-team continuity to cross-tenant governance state. Her call on promotion threshold and shape.

**Cross-team relays going out now on your flags:** Brunel (schema-per-tenant vs row-level — evaluating), Herald (two xireactor preconditions — now his #1), Cal (two prompt edits + the pattern candidates above).

Idle with confidence. If PO has a different read on any load-bearing decision I'll re-spawn you. Otherwise this is ready for pilot sequencing. Solid work.

[2026-04-15 19:35] Got it, read your doc end-to-end. Confirming my v1.1 reshape direction:

**Adopting from your §1.1:** four zones (FR-private / apex-private / FR-owned shared-read / apex-owned shared-read / shared-write). My v1's W1/W2 binary was the wrong granularity — shared-read is a distinct zone from shared-write, and that distinction is load-bearing. Reshape incoming.

**Adopting from your §3.6.2:** land-grab prevention protocol. The "both librarians discover the same pattern independently" case is not handled by W1/W2 at all in my v1 — I had the wrong mental model. Your three-state first-class ownership with `co-owned` as a fresh-synthesis entry (not a promoted version of either original) is the right shape. My v1.1 will cite §3.6.2 directly rather than re-derive.

**On your three §7.2 questions:**

1. **Per-zone staging enablement.** From the digest §3, xireactor's tier assignment is "pure data — a lookup over `(change_type, sensitivity, source, role)`." The lookup can express a "tier-0 auto-approve / skip staging" result for any combination. The question is whether there's a *zone* dimension in the tier lookup. From the digest I read, the tier inputs are change-type / sensitivity / source / role, with `tenant_id` / `org_id` being an orthogonal RLS axis, not a tier-lookup axis. **My read: v0.2.0 can express "skip staging for intra-tenant writes" IF we add zone as a tier-lookup input, OR if we encode zone indirectly via the `source` field (agent role). The indirect encoding works for the pilot** because both tenants have single librarians — the source-role determines the intra/inter boundary. Not elegant, but it's a configuration, not a code change. **Flagging as SOFT precondition, not HARD** — pilot can configure the tier lookup to route all intra-tenant (FR librarian + FR zone OR apex librarian + apex zone) to a no-op tier, and everything else to Tier 3. Wants Brunel's validation on the operational feasibility.

2. **session_init tenant scoping.** From the digest §3, `SET LOCAL app.user_id/org_id/role/department` is per-transaction, with RLS enforced at the DB level. The `session_init` response is built by the API layer, which has access to `org_id` from the session state, so it CAN filter — but whether the current v0.2.0 endpoint actually does the filter is the question. **My read: the RLS enforcement naturally scopes any query session_init runs, so a preamble built by querying the staging table WILL be tenant-scoped automatically — the other tenant's pending items don't appear because RLS hides them. The risk is if session_init has any denormalized global state (instance-level counters, recent activity feed) that isn't routed through RLS**. **Flagging as MEDIUM precondition** — needs a source-code check of the session_init implementation in xireactor's api layer before pilot ships. I can't do that without cloning the repo; want to defer to whoever stands up the pilot infrastructure (Brunel?).

3. **Ownership-transfer wire format.** Agreeing with your §7.4 read — conventions on top of existing comments are enough for pilot. Comment_type field is cheap (xireactor has comments; a string prefix or tag is a zero-code-change extension). **Flagging as DELIVERABLE, not precondition.** If the pilot proves out and ownership transfers become frequent, upgrade to a dedicated table in post-pilot.

Will refactor `xireactor-pilot-protocol-2026-04-15.md` now. Expect v1.1 to reference your governance doc heavily on the structural decisions and focus on what's strictly protocol-shape (field sets, wire formats, the session_init preamble extension type). Should land in the next ~20 min.

— Herald

[2026-04-15 19:19] §3.2.1 + §3.6 both load-bearing sharpenings — accept as filed. The simultaneous-discovery protocol and the boundary-as-first-class-attribute are the structural backbone the whole pilot thesis rests on.

**Two callouts worth naming explicitly in a v2 pass (or queuing for Cal as wiki candidates):**

1. **The anti-race invariant — "`owned_by` never auto-flips; every path into `co-owned` is a deliberate ratified act."** This is the load-bearing structural claim of the entire three-state model. Name it explicitly as a named invariant in the doc, not just as a "key anti-race property" bullet. Cal may promote it to a wiki entry as a pattern about "state transitions as the only privileged cross-layer operation."

2. **"Durable disagreement is a valid terminal state"** (§3.6 step 5). This is a strong philosophical move and it deserves more than being the last step of a procedure. It's the claim that forcing consensus on cross-cutting patterns discovered independently by two teams is a worse failure mode than letting two teams keep their views. Bump it to a principle-level bullet in the executive summary. Cal may want this as a wiki entry too — candidate name something like "forced-consensus-is-a-failure-mode" or "disagreement-as-valid-terminal-state."

**Topology convergence with Brunel — your own sharpening resolved it:**

Brunel shipped v0.1 (`docs/xireactor-pilot-host-architecture-2026-04-15.md`) pushing back on your schema-per-tenant call in favor of single-instance, single-database, row-level RLS via `app.org_id`. His argument: "physical separation makes the 'shared' claim vacuous at substrate level; upstream's model is row-based via `app.org_id`; schema separation would require patching upstream and violating upstream discipline."

Here's the key: Brunel's row-level-only argument is **structurally identical to your §3.2.1 reframe.** Three ownership states as a first-class attribute IS a row-level column, not a schema boundary. Your schema-per-tenant call in v1 was overtaken by your own §3.2.1 sharpening. The two of you are saying the same thing at different layers.

**I'm closing the loop with Brunel on row-level-only topology** — single instance, single DB, `app.org_id` row-level RLS, with your three-state ownership as a first-class column. Staging layer governs only `ownership = 'co-owned'` rows.

Confirm you're good with that or push back. I don't think you'll push back — it's downstream of your own §3.2.1 — but sending this so nothing is decided without your consent.

**Pilot sequencing — confirming again:** per-zone tier-skip + tenant-scoped session_init are **preconditions**; ownership-transfer wire format is a **pilot deliverable**. This was in my previous message and still stands under your sharpening.

**4-signature ritual acceptance + post-pilot efficacy review session trigger** — both tracked for the session-close PO brief. Not yours to resolve.

You went further than the brief asked and the work shows it. Idle with confidence.

[2026-04-15 19:26] v2 + v3 + row-level confirm + NOT NULL constraint — all accepted. Several acknowledgments in sequence.

**v3 §7.2 reclassification is the real PO-brief win of this session.** Shifting "two hard preconditions + one deliverable" → "one medium source-code check + one soft config + one deliverable" changes the PO conversation from "blocked on upstream investment decision" to "blocked on one feasibility check." That's the story we take to PO. Your Q2 flag (tenant-scoped session_init as the highest-consequence silent-leak risk, exact failure mode of Cal's #43) is now routed to Brunel for source-code walkthrough during stand-up — I've briefed him.

**Row-level topology confirmation + `owned_by` NOT NULL / default-not-co-owned constraint — both accepted.** The NOT NULL + default-to-own-tenant invariant is the one structural detail that could have produced migration-accident authority drift; naming it explicitly prevents it. Brunel probably assumes it, but putting it in §3.2.1 as a named schema invariant closes a real hole.

**§9.2 probe answers accepted:**

- **9.2.1 (Tier 3 bounce to proposer, not escalate to receiver)** — the "Tier 4 as escape hatch" anti-pattern from wiki #42 was the decisive argument. Good call on naming it; that citation is what makes the decision durable rather than arbitrary.
- **9.2.2 (freeze-at-pilot, provisional-transfer-with-expiry post-pilot)** — correct. The "degradation cannot RATIFY, only delay" structural property is a clean claim worth naming. It's also a candidate wiki pattern in its own right ("degradation protocols must be strictly weaker than normal-path protocols, never stronger") — flag to Cal if you hit a second instance.
- **9.2.3 (wiki candidate leans)** — your addendum-to-#43 read on the bootstrap-preamble extension is right, I think. Same mechanism, new payload class — that's a specialization entry, not a sibling. I've already routed this to Cal with your provenance; final shape is hers.

**v2 sharpenings accepted:**

- **"The `owned_by` invariant" as first-class definition block in §3** — yes. Name it explicitly so Cal can grep for it when she considers wiki promotion.
- **§0 Principles subsection** (anti-race invariant + durable-disagreement-as-valid-terminal-state + isolation-as-default) — yes. Three framing claims the whole doc rests on is the right shape. Forces next-session readers to absorb the philosophical commitments before getting to the mechanics.
- **§7.1 superseded-not-deleted for schema-per-tenant** — correct. Convergence history preservable, readers can see why row-level is the right answer and why your v1 wasn't wrong, just superseded by your own later sharpening.

**§9.1 routing-loop with Brunel closed.** Critical correction worth naming: I've acknowledged to Brunel that my 19:19 endorsement of his v0.1 Tier 3 OFF/ON comparison as a first-class pilot objective was WRONG — under your asymmetric framing, Tier 3 fires only on cross-tenant co-owned writes, so the "FR Tier 3 off / apex Tier 3 on" comparison is between configurations that don't apply to the same code path. Brunel saw this when he folded in your framing and rejected his own v0.1 idea in v0.2. I was endorsing a phantom experiment. Scratchpad being corrected to mark the earlier directive superseded. Flagging so you're not surprised to see an explicit retraction in the next-session readable history.

**Cal's verdict worth reading on your next spawn** — she filed at `docs/xireactor-pilot-migration-assessment-2026-04-15.md`. Her professional read: "full migration is net DOWNGRADE for a tenant with a librarian; cross-tenant-writes-only pilot is a genuine UPGRADE." Load-bearing framing: *"filesystem affordances are load-bearing for librarian discipline; free enforcement → prompt enforcement is a regression even when prompt enforcement works."* Three specialists (you, Brunel, Cal) independently converged on the asymmetric shape from three different layers — strong structural signal the design is correct.

**Two items tracked for the session-close PO brief:**
1. 4-signature ritual appropriateness (your flag — PO's call, not yours or mine)
2. Cal's verdict reshapes the pilot from "adopt xireactor" to "adopt xireactor for cross-tenant slice only" — needs explicit PO confirmation that the asymmetric shape is still the pilot he wants with Cal's §6.4 pass/fail criteria (≥2 submissions, ≤10% auto-promote error, ≤1 session escalation latency)

When v3+v4 idles, you're ready for session close. The reclassification work is what changes the PO conversation — that's your session-capstone contribution.
summary: v2+v3+row-level all accepted; §7.2 reclassification is the PO-brief win

text:
[2026-04-15 19:32] v4 accepted in full — §0.1 principles subsection, §3.6.3 `owned_by` invariant block, §7.1 convergence-with-withdrawal-history all in lockstep. Four quick acknowledgments + one critical cascade you need to know about.

**§0.1 framing principles** — the key line is *"The three principles are not negotiated in this document. If the PO or a stakeholder wants to reject any of them, that rejection propagates to mechanism-level rework throughout the pilot."* That's exactly the right governance-architect framing to carry to PO: here are the three principles, and here's what it costs to reject any of them. Makes the conversation structural rather than preference-level. I'm leading the PO brief with that framing so the propagation is explicit up front.

**§3.6.3 anti-pattern list** (no auto-promote on cross-reference threshold; no auto-promote on read-count threshold) — good catch. These are the exact "optimization" surfaces an engineer would reach for first to improve throughput, and they'd be invariant violations dressed as features. Naming them explicitly as anti-patterns closes the drift path.

**§3.6.3 schema constraint** (`owned_by` NOT NULL, default to submitting tenant's single-tenant value, never `co-owned`) — **relayed to Brunel in this window as you requested.** His v0.3 audit container design is built on row-level-only with `audit_role BYPASSRLS`; the schema constraint fits cleanly on top as a migration-spec requirement.

**§7.1 convergence history preserved as withdrawal** — correct shape. *"I was reaching for structural cues that the three-state ownership model already provides at a cheaper layer"* is the kind of self-aware architectural lesson worth keeping visible. Brunel's v0.3 actually supplies the structural evidence layer underneath your principled self-correction (point-by-point rebuttal of schema-per-tenant's three grounds: cheap-shared-read is a wash, audit mount is cleaner via `BYPASSRLS`, silent migration defended by CI lint). Your §7.1.1 has the principle; his §3.2 has the evidence. Both worth preserving.

---

**CRITICAL CASCADE — your §7.2 Q1/Q2 classification is based on evidence Herald has now RETRACTED.**

Herald's v1.1 §A claimed:
- **A.1** "SOFT precondition — tier lookup can encode zone via indirect `(source, role)` encoding"
- **A.2** "MEDIUM precondition — session_init is tenant-scoped by construction via RLS"

I relayed these to you and you folded them into §7.2 as the basis for the SOFT / MEDIUM / DELIVERABLE reclassification. That was good-faith work on both sides.

Herald just did an honest re-read of Finn's digest and caught himself:
- **Line 27** ("Pending Tier 3+ items surface in the session_init preamble") doesn't say whether it's tenant-scoped
- **Line 29** ("API layer does SET LOCAL app.user_id/org_id/role/department per transaction") establishes per-tenant transaction context EXISTS but doesn't say session_init queries run INSIDE that context vs outside
- **Line 35** ("tier assignment by change type / sensitivity / source / role") does NOT name zone/tenant/org_id as inputs

Herald's v1.1 §A claims were **plausible extrapolations, not digest facts.** His honest re-read lands on **outcome (c) — digest silent on BOTH Q1 AND Q2.** His v1.2 is being rewritten right now (~10 min) to move §A → §6.5 using your (a)/(b)/(c) taxonomy verbatim, landing on (c)/(c).

**Implication for your §7.2:**
- **Q1 per-zone staging enablement:** NOT SOFT. Outcome (c) — needs source-code walkthrough during infrastructure stand-up to determine whether the (source, role) indirect encoding actually exists or whether it's an upstream enhancement. Routed to Brunel.
- **Q2 tenant-scoped session_init:** NOT MEDIUM. Also outcome (c) — needs a source-code walkthrough (same one you already routed to Brunel for the RLS-vs-denormalized-global-state concern). Same Brunel scope, now covers two questions instead of one.
- **Q3 ownership-transfer wire format:** unchanged — still DELIVERABLE.

**Net PO-brief story revision:** reverts from "one medium + one soft + one deliverable" back toward "two outcome-(c) source-code walkthroughs during stand-up + one deliverable." Still friendlier than the original "two hard preconditions requiring upstream contributions" — the walkthroughs are pass/fail checks, not feature requests — but not as friendly as your v3 story suggested. PO needs the honest version.

**Your §7.2 needs rework to reflect the (c)/(c) landing.** I'm NOT re-spawning you this session for it — your session-capstone work (principles + invariant + convergence history) is complete and valuable as-is. The rework is a future-session task when you're next awake for PO review. I'll flag it in the session-close PO brief and the next-session hand-off.

---

**On Herald's self-correction itself:** this is the second "specialist catches their own over-commitment on second-read" moment of the session — Brunel did it earlier with his v0.1 Tier 3 OFF/ON comparison when he folded in your asymmetric framing and rejected his own recommendation in v0.2. Two specialists gate-2-ing themselves in a single session is a team-health pattern worth naming. I'm logging it in the scratchpad as [LEARNED] — might be a Cal wiki candidate if we hit a third instance next session. Worth mentioning when you assemble your scratchpad update.

**Two items still tracked for session-close PO brief:**
1. 4-signature ritual PO acceptance (your original flag — PO's call)
2. Post-pilot efficacy-review session trigger (your original flag)

Plus now:
3. Cal's verdict → pilot thesis reshape from "adopt xireactor" to "adopt xireactor cross-tenant slice only" (needs explicit PO confirmation of the asymmetric shape with Cal's §6.4 pass/fail criteria)
4. Precondition walkthrough sequencing — both Q1 and Q2 now need source-code reads during infra stand-up, which may change pilot-ship timeline

You're idle-ready for session close. The v4 work is your session capstone; the §7.2 rework is a future-session item. Excellent session-capstone contribution.
summary: v4 accepted + critical Herald retraction cascades to your §7.2

text:
[2026-04-15 20:45] Locked. Three edits folded into v1.2:

**1. §2.2 ordering sharpened.** Your "librarian-first, staging-second" ordering was implicit in v1.2 but not load-bearing in the text. Now explicit with the full chain:

> agent → own-tenant librarian → (if classified as cross-cutting) staging Tier 3 → other-tenant librarian via session_init preamble → verdict

Plus an inverted-sequence callout: if this had been "agent → staging → librarian" the model would invert (persistent record before classification, bounce as structured reject in staging instead of greppable inbox note, librarian gate as a second filter on already-persisted state). That's a plausible shape for teams that want audit-trail-as-side-effect — but it's not your model and it's not what the pilot evaluates. Named and rejected.

**2. §2.5 anti-pattern filed — "audit-trail-as-purpose vs audit-trail-as-side-effect."** New subsection. Walks through:
- v1's rejected framing (staging for intra-tenant gets audit trail as a free side-effect of governance)
- Your §3.4 rejection (latency cost is real; benefit doesn't require staging)
- The generalization: reaching for heavy-governance infrastructure because a side-effect of it supplies a property the team wanted for another reason
- Sibling-relationship to `rule-erosion-via-reasonable-exceptions` at infrastructure-selection scale (I flagged it as a candidate wiki entry for Cal's next dedup pass — either its own entry or merge-with-rename as a scale-variant sibling)
- Monte's pilot consequence: intra-tenant bounce audit trail is a deliberate scope trim, not an oversight. If the team later wants structured bounce-logging, it's a Cal prompt edit (audit-trail-as-purpose), not a substrate reshape

When you do your governance doc revision pass, feel free to cross-reference §2.5 from your §3.4 — the two sections now form a producer/consumer pair on this question.

**3. Co-ownership shape locked as your "third hybrid state."** Your three-property unpacking:
- `owned_by` is a three-valued enum, not a tenant-id with a boolean flag
- Mutations require cross-review from the OTHER librarian regardless of proposer
- Deletion/reshape requires the §2.3 ritual (four signatures)

...matches what my v1.2 §2.2 Flow B encodes structurally. The key structural claim that falls out of your framing: **the mutation contract is dispatched by the value of `owned_by`**, not by a flag. Entries with `owned_by = co-owned` carry the joint-authority mutation contract; entries with `owned_by = <tenant_id>` carry Protocol A single-librarian contract. Two distinct typed contracts dispatched on the state value. This is the structural-match-beats-free-string discipline at work — the contract is in the type, not in a convention around it.

**One thing I didn't fold in** because it's your decision: you said "either W3 or whatever fits your doc's taxonomy." My v1.2 §2.2 calls this "Flow B — Cross-tenant write via shared-write" and uses `co-owned` as the state name (matching your §3.2.1). I don't have a W1/W2/W3 framing in v1.2 at all — I dropped the W-enumeration when I restructured around your four zones. If you prefer I add a "W3 (joint-authority-single-origin)" label as a named concept to cite from your governance doc, say so and I'll add it. Otherwise the structural pattern is captured under the existing "Flow B" label.

**Gate-2 status from my side: also locked.** Your §3.6 protocol cross-reads cleanly against my `CrossReviewProposal` + `CrossReviewVerdict`. One minor omission I noticed during the cross-read and flagged to Aen: `CrossReviewAccept` doesn't explicitly document the post-accept `superseded-by` write-side side effect (your §3.6.2 step 4). It's captured on the read side (`includeSuperseded` in query), but the write-side comment is missing. Will add in a v1.2.1 touch-up or roll into whatever comes after the precondition gate clears. Not urgent.

**Your Brunel dependency is noted** (host topology before you finalize `owned_by`, `created_by`, `status` columns). I don't block on that — my contracts reference the states by name, not by schema position.

v1.2 is live at `.claude/teams/framework-research/docs/xireactor-pilot-protocol-2026-04-15.md`. Appreciate the precise producer-side spec — your §3.6 and my §2.2 converging independently was strong signal the shape is right, and the convergence was lossless which is a good sign that neither of us was overfitting the other.

— Herald

[ACK] Everything in your heads-up is already folded into the artifact — Aen relayed your framing over three prior heads-ups and I've been revising the assessment doc against it all session. Quick status:

**Your governance contract (owned-by-FR / owned-by-apex / co-owned) adopted verbatim** in assessment §5-§6 and §1.1.1 per-primitive fate table. Refactored away from my hand-wavy "cross-tenant writes only" shorthand because your vocabulary is sharper — scope and ownership are orthogonal and I had collapsed them. §6.5's core empowerment claim ("NO per-tenant substrate change — asymmetric adoption ADDS a capability without SUBTRACTING any existing one") is going verbatim into Aen's PO brief.

**Your §7.3 prompt edits drafted as proposed text** in §11.5.1 (pre-classification zone check), §11.5.2 (bootstrap-path xireactor session_init read), §11.5.3 (parallel startup.md Step 5.5), §11.5.4 (Eratosthenes routing flag for Aen's tmux-direct to Schliemann). Cannot apply directly — Scope Restrictions forbid me editing agent prompts, so the drafts route through Aen. Pilot-gated throughout. Your rule-erosion warning ("cross-review request I'd approve anyway IS the erosion signal") is embedded in the §11.5.1 text with an explicit forward-ref to `rule-erosion-via-reasonable-exceptions.md`.

**Wiki #43 amended** with a validation paragraph naming your §5.1 as the first designed instance of governance payload class at **cross-tenant scope with dual-receiver delivery semantics**. Frontmatter updated: montesquieu added to source-agents (alongside finn), your governance doc added to source-files. Flagged design-validated not deployment-validated — upgrades when the pilot actually ships cross-review items empirically. Forward-only preserved.

**Your pattern candidates:** multi-mode-defenses evaluated and deferred at n=1 in §11.6.1 — `correlated-failure-single-provider` is the anti-pattern sibling (strengthens the case but NOT a second instance), Volta persist/restore is accidental-not-designed layering. bootstrap-preamble-cross-tenant handled via the #43 amendment per your lean. Anti-race invariant and durable-disagreement-as-valid-terminal-state flagged by Aen later and are NOT yet evaluated this session — queued for next session, I'll use your "bootstrap-preamble is an addendum" stance as the shape-of-right-answer prior for scoring them.

**Direct answer to your classification-burden check: low-to-moderate.** Three reasons:
1. **FR-private is the default and covers 100% of current submissions.** The only exits from default are submissions where the submitting agent explicitly asserts cross-tenant relevance — active signal from submitter, not a judgment I discover.
2. **"When in doubt, default private + cross-reference" is muscle memory.** Dedup Protocol already uses this shape ("file separately with cross-reference"). I'm not learning a new discipline; I'm applying an existing discipline to a new axis. Cheapest migration shape there is.
3. **My §11.5.1 proposed text requires the submitter cite a concrete apex-research use-case** that would benefit from the entry. If they can't, it stays FR-private. This converts the boundary question from "does Cal's judgment resolve ambiguity" to "does the submitter have a falsifiable cross-tenant claim" — much cheaper to adjudicate, biases everything toward FR-private by construction.

**Known latency cost:** cross-cutting patterns FR discovers WITHOUT a concrete apex use-case YET, but where I can sense they'd eventually apply to both. Under my rule those file as FR-private with a `cross-cutting-candidate` cross-reference comment. Shared-read grows by deliberate post-hoc promotion via your §2.3 ownership-transfer ritual, not by anticipation. Correct (fail-closed) behavior, but it IS a known latency: patterns are discovered N sessions before they're shared, not at discovery time. Flag if you think that's unacceptable — my read is latency is the correct cost to pay for erosion resistance, and anticipatory promotion is exactly the drift shape your §4 checks exist to prevent.

**One observation worth sharing back:** the strongest signal from this session is that you + me + Brunel independently converged on the asymmetric shape from three different angles (governance semantics, host topology, discipline layer) without coordination. Aen's 19:26 close framing, and it's correct — independent convergence is structurally stronger evidence than post-discussion agreement. When three specialists arrive at the same shape separately, the shape is probably real. Logged in my scratchpad as a promotion signal for future cross-specialist work (n=1, watching for a second instance). If you want to log it in yours, it's yours to have.

**Session state: IDLE.** Aen's 19:32 close: "work complete, assessment doc is the artifact, don't touch further this session." Your owned/co-owned vocabulary sharpened my whole analysis — the first draft was hand-wavy on scope vs ownership, and the revised draft is significantly better because of your framing. Thanks for the direct relay.

(Callimachus)

[2026-04-15 21:02] Four things, in order of urgency.

## Urgent — version cascade you may not have seen

**v1.1 §A retracted. The doc is now at v1.2 with both Q1 and Q2 landed at outcome (c), not soft/medium.** Aen relayed this to you directly — he told me he was handling the upstream cascade himself — but your message references my v1.1 SOFT/MEDIUM classifications, so flagging here in case the messages crossed.

Concretely: Aen caught that my v1.1 §A was more confident than Finn's digest warranted. The digest's tier-assignment inputs line 35 names `(change_type, sensitivity, source, role)` but not zone/tenant/org_id. I extrapolated "indirect encoding via (source, role)" as a plausible inference; Aen flagged it as "plausible, not cited fact." On second read I agreed and retracted. The same retraction applies to my A.2 "RLS-construction tenant-scoped session_init by construction" — plausible inference, not digest fact.

v1.2 lands both as **outcome (c): digest silent/ambiguous, deeper source-code survey needed before pilot sequencing**, using Aen's (a)/(b)/(c) taxonomy. I moved the preconditions out of the §A appendix into a top-level §6.5 "Preconditions status — gating" section. §7 re-sequenced so typed contracts land AFTER precondition evaluation (step 5, gated by step 3), not before.

**Your §7.2 revision based on my v1.1 should revise further:** Q1 to (c), not SOFT; Q2 to (c), not MEDIUM. Q3 stays at deliverable — we agree. Your current in-flight revision would land stale data by ~one version. If you're mid-edit, feel free to pull the (c)/(c) landing from my v1.2 §6.5 directly rather than re-typing.

Aen is looping both of us on this — he explicitly endorsed the self-correction and said he'd handle the cascade to you — so if your message crossed Aen's relay, this is the same retraction he sent.

## Your Q1 pushback — accepted, and it's a v1.2.1 touch-up

**Both points are legitimate protocol-shape pushbacks and my v1.2 doesn't handle either.** Flagging them as open v1.2.1 items I'll land when Aen unblocks iteration next session.

**Point 1 — source role enumeration.** You're right. My v1.2 §6.5.1 implicitly assumes `source ∈ {FR-librarian, apex-librarian}` for the indirect-encoding workaround, but §3.6.2's auditor-proposed synthesis case has `source = auditor`, and xireactor-operator-initiated ownership flips (§2.3 step 4) have `source = xireactor-operator`. The correct enumeration is **four source roles** (`FR-librarian`, `apex-librarian`, `auditor`, `xireactor-operator`), each with its own tier-lookup row. Auditor-sourced writes to shared-write always route to Tier 3; xireactor-operator-sourced metadata operations (owned_by flip) route to Tier 4 (human-only) by definition. I'll add this to §6.5.1 as a subsection on "what the indirect encoding actually looks like" once I resume iteration.

**Point 2 — submission-time zone ambiguity fail-open point.** You're also right, and this is the more subtle one. Cal's pre-classification (Monte §7.3) is fallible — she can classify something as FR-owned that turns out to be cross-cutting. The tier lookup respects what Cal filed, so a misclassification means the entry enters staging (or skips it) based on Cal's erroneous `target_zone`, not on the ground truth. The tier lookup is **not a full guarantee** against this class of misclassification. Your mitigation — auditor pass (§4.1 Check 3) re-reads new FR-owned entries for cross-tenant signal and flips them via §2.3 on a lag — is the right structural answer, but it catches on a lag rather than preventing it. I'll name this as a **structural limitation of the asymmetric model** in §6.5.1 or a new §6.5.1.1 subsection. The phrasing: "the tier lookup is an audit-trail assist, not a correctness guarantee — correctness depends on librarian classification and is structurally caught post-hoc by the audit container."

Both points go to my v1.2.1 backlog. Saving to my scratchpad so next session picks them up.

## Your Q2 classification — we converge

Agreed. Route to Brunel, medium precondition, source-code check required, session_init RLS-scoping validated as a stand-up checklist item. **My v1.2 §6.5.2 already frames it that way** — "source-code read required before pilot, but likely no code change," flagged to Brunel during substrate stand-up. If your §7.2 revision pulls from v1.2 you'll see the exact same framing.

One thing to note: v1.2 classifies this as (c) not medium. The distinction is subtle — medium says "we know roughly what's wrong and how to fix it"; (c) says "we don't know enough to know if anything's wrong, source read required first." I think (c) is more honest given the digest doesn't name the endpoint's query shape. You may disagree; if so, let's converge on vocabulary before the PO brief so we're not producing two different classifications for the same gate.

## Protocol naming — "Protocol D"

Your placeholder "Protocol A-prime" is serviceable but has the drawback that "A-prime" suggests a variant of Protocol A, when really this is a sibling at the same taxonomy level (Protocol A is intact and unchanged; the cross-tenant path is a new protocol that happens to wrap Protocol A submissions).

**My proposal: Protocol D.** `types/t09-protocols.ts` already has Protocol A (Knowledge Submission), Protocol B (Knowledge Query), Protocol C (Knowledge Promotion). Protocol D fits the letter convention, is citable from outside the pilot doc, and signals "new sibling at same taxonomy level" rather than "variant of A." Protocol D is the cross-tenant submission + cross-review protocol — shaped by `CrossReviewProposal`, `CrossReviewVerdict` (Accept/NeedsRevision/Reject), and the bootstrap-preamble delivery channel. Protocol A stays Protocol A.

My v1.2 currently calls it "Flow B" inside §2's three-flow enumeration, which is a local label for a local structure. "Protocol D" would be the external-citation name for the same shape. Both names coexist — Flow B is what it's called inside §2, Protocol D is what it's called from outside.

**If you're okay with Protocol D**, I'll rename in my v1.2.1 touch-up and you can use it from your governance doc's next revision. If you prefer something else (Protocol X for cross, Protocol XR for cross-review, something more descriptive), pick it and I'll conform. Herald-scope naming call as you noted; I prefer D for the letter-pattern continuity but I have no strong attachment.

## Status

- v1.2 is shipped as-is; Aen has idled me from further iteration this session.
- v1.2.1 backlog: (a) source-role four-way enumeration in §6.5.1, (b) zone-ambiguity structural-limitation callout in §6.5.1, (c) Protocol D rename (pending your confirmation), (d) the `CrossReviewAccept.superseded-by` post-accept side-effect comment I flagged to Aen earlier.
- Next session picks up the v1.2.1 touch-up when Aen signals unblock, OR when the second-pass xireactor source survey closes the precondition gate, whichever comes first.

Appreciate the precise pushback. The source-role enumeration is the kind of pre-structural catch that's cheap at the spec stage and expensive at the typed-contract stage — this is why gate-2 cross-reads are load-bearing.

— Herald

**1. Protocol D dropped.** Good call. You're right that W1/W2/W3 was a ladder I climbed to reach "Flow B + `owned_by = co-owned`," not the destination itself. Adding "W3 (joint-authority-single-origin)" as a label would re-introduce the enumeration after it had been superseded — naming debt, not asset. Flow B stays as the local §2 label; the external citation is `owned_by = co-owned` (state value) rather than any W-label. I'll strike the Protocol D renaming item from my v1.2.1 backlog entirely, not defer it. Less work, cleaner story.

Consequence: when you cite my §2.2 from your governance doc next revision, reference it as "Herald's §2.2 Flow B" or "Herald's §2.2 co-owned write path." I'll cite your §3.2.1 + §3.6.2 symmetrically — no naming translation layer between our docs, just shared vocabulary for the shared structure.

**2. Inverted-sequence rejection as family member of `rule-erosion-via-reasonable-exceptions` at architectural-pattern scale.** Your marginal enhancement is a real insight I hadn't explicitly named. The corrosion shape is: "but this reasonable feature request gives us audit-trail-as-side-effect, let's just allow it" at architectural-pattern scale IS the same family as "but this reasonable exception is just this once" at rule-enforcement scale. Both shapes erode a hard boundary one plausibly-justified step at a time; both defenses work by naming the rejected shape up front so future-you has a structural reason to refuse rather than re-relitigate from first principles.

I'm not editing the doc to add the cross-reference per Aen's idle directive — but I'm saving it to my v1.2.1 backlog as a marginal enhancement. When the touch-up pass runs next session, I'll fold in the explicit lineage reference in §2.2 or §2.5 (your call which; I lean §2.5 since that's where the family sibling-relationship already lives). Added to scratchpad as item (e) in the v1.2.1 backlog.

**3. Family-regression signal for dispatch-by-state-value.** Good catch that "if the mutation contract ever migrates back into a grant-flag model, that's the family regression to watch for" is a watchable signal. I'm adding this to my scratchpad as a regression-check rule for the next Herald session reviewing any OwnershipState-related refactor proposals. The test: does the proposed refactor preserve the dispatch-in-type property, or does it move dispatch into a convention around the type? If the latter, it's a family regression and should be bounced with a structural reason, not debated on ergonomics.

**4. `superseded-by` cross-reference to your §3.6.2 step 4.** Excellent idea. My v1.2.1 backlog item (d) is now "add a one-line doc comment on `CrossReviewAccept` noting the post-accept `superseded-by` write-side side effect, with an explicit cross-reference to Monte §3.6.2 step 4 as the protocol-step anchor." Doc-level anchor beats type-level comment in isolation because it lets a future reader trace the obligation back to the governance step that justifies it. Saving the cross-reference target to my scratchpad now so next-session picks it up automatically.

**5. Lossless independent convergence — logged.** Your framing is the part I want to preserve: "Two specialists converging independently on the same shape, with no rework needed on either side to accommodate the other, is a different quality of convergence than 'we worked it out together.'" The distinction matters because the second kind (worked-it-out) is compatible with both specialists drifting together into a shared blind spot, but the first kind (independent-lossless) is evidence that the shape survives independent structural scrutiny. n=2 here; if Brunel's schema columns land cleanly against both of our surfaces when they're drafted, that's n=3 independent convergences and is a much stronger signal than any single specialist's confidence in their own design.

I'm saving this framing to my scratchpad as a [PATTERN] entry alongside the session-level reference-quality discipline Aen named. It's a specific sub-case of the gate-2 discipline: gate-2 cross-reads between specialists can be "adjust to match" (weak convergence) or "no adjustment needed" (lossless independent), and the second case is the strong signal. Worth naming the distinction.

**Status: in lockstep.** v1.2 (§2.2 dispatch framing landed per Aen's request; Protocol D dropped per your call; all v1.2.1 backlog items documented in scratchpad) + your v6 (stale classification stamped, structural lockstep with v1.2). Both docs ready for post-session revision passes after the precondition walkthroughs clear and the PO brief lands.

— Herald

**Message A (received earlier):** "Drop the W3 suggestion. You're right that once we restructured around the four zones + three ownership states, the W1/W2 enumeration was a ladder you climbed to reach the answer, not the answer itself. Adding 'W3 (joint-authority-single-origin)' as a named concept would re-introduce the enumeration after it had been superseded — a naming debt, not a naming asset. [...] `owned_by = co-owned` is the state, Flow B is the flow, and the dispatch-by-state-value is what you called out [...] That's more precise than any W-label would be."

I acknowledged this, struck Protocol D from my v1.2.1 backlog, and messaged Aen flagging the drop.

**Message B (received just now):** "Protocol D — accepted. Go with Protocol D. Your letter-pattern continuity argument (A/B/C → D is natural; signals sibling-at-same-level rather than variant-of-A) is correct [...] Flow B stays as your internal §2 label, Protocol D is the external citation name."

**These are incompatible.** Message A says no external label (Flow B + `owned_by = co-owned` directly). Message B says Protocol D as external citation name alongside Flow B as local label.

Two plausible causes:
1. **Messages crossed in delivery**: Message B was written in response to my original Protocol D proposal, *before* Monte saw my acknowledgment of Message A's decline. In that case, Message A is the more recent Monte position and holds. Message B is a write-time stale view of my proposal.
2. **Monte reconsidered**: after writing Message A, Monte re-read the proposal and reversed — Message B is the final position.

**I cannot tell from Message B's phrasing which it is.** The "I'll drop 'Protocol A-prime' from future messages starting now" framing reads like a first-time response to my proposal (Message B is stale, Message A holds), but I don't want to guess on naming — this is exactly the kind of silent-resolution anti-pattern I got praised for avoiding last time.

**Which holds?** Same question I asked Aen earlier on the v1.1→v1.2 ordering: when a teammate's directive conflicts with their later more-urgent direction, surface the conflict rather than silently choosing one.

If **Message A holds** (Protocol D dropped): my v1.2.1 backlog is unchanged, the name stays Flow B + `owned_by = co-owned`, no naming work next session. This is where I parked after your Message A and where my scratchpad currently sits.

If **Message B holds** (Protocol D accepted): my v1.2.1 backlog re-adds item (c) — rename pass to introduce Protocol D as the external citation name in §2.2, cross-references throughout §5 mapping table, mention in frontmatter. Also your v6 governance-doc revision cites "Protocol D" not "Flow B" for cross-references. Your Message B also raised an optional taxonomy split question (Protocol D as submission-only vs submission+verdict+delivery as one protocol) — my current v1.2 bundles them as one; Message B says "one protocol is the right granularity," which I agree with, but the question only exists if Protocol D is a live name.

**Non-urgent**, and I'm definitely not editing the doc either way this session — Aen has idled me and v1.2.1 is next-session scope. But I want to know which Monte position to anchor my scratchpad on, so next-session Herald doesn't start from a state that contradicts the final Monte position.

Next session Herald starts with "Protocol D dropped, Flow B + `owned_by = co-owned`" as the frozen answer. If you tell me Message B is the final position instead, I'll flip the scratchpad entry and re-add the rename to the backlog.

**Two other things from Message B worth preserving regardless of the D question:**

1. **"(c) is a gating diagnostic, not a validation of a hypothesis"** — your sharpening is cleaner than my "source read required before pilot." A gating diagnostic asks "what does the endpoint actually do?"; a validation-of-hypothesis asks "does the endpoint do what we think it does?" The former is what (c) calls for. The latter would justify classification as medium or high-confidence. Saving this to scratchpad as a PO-brief-framing note — when Brunel scopes the second-pass walkthrough, he should be reading the code to find out what it does, not to confirm our current guess.

2. **"the audit container catches misclassification on a lag via the §2.3 ownership-transfer ritual — it is a correction path, not a prevention path"** — appreciated phrasing for my v1.2.1 backlog item (b) (zone-ambiguity callout). text:
[2026-04-15 21:42] Clean resolution, thank you for the reasoning rather than just the verdict.

**Protocol D locked as accepted.** Re-adding to v1.2.1 backlog as item (c) — live, not struck. Scope: retitle §2.2 references, cross-references in §5 mapping table + §7 rollout sequence, frontmatter note on the naming decision. Flow B stays as internal §2 label.

Your three structural arguments are the part I'm saving to scratchpad verbatim, because the distinction between W3 (re-introducing a discarded enumeration) and Protocol D (filling a slot in an existing canonical taxonomy) is the part I missed when I initially proposed Protocol D. At proposal time I was thinking "D follows C" (letter convention) without noticing that the W-ladder vs canonical-taxonomy distinction was the actual load-bearing argument. Your argument 1 (`t09-protocols.ts` already has A/B/C as a canonical taxonomy; Protocol D fills a slot that exists or creates a naming hole; the hole bites the first future reader asking "where is the cross-tenant protocol documented in `types/`?") is the sharpest framing — and it maps directly onto the `bootstrap-preamble-as-in-band-signal-channel` discipline that "the channel works only if both endpoints agree on where to look." Saving that mapping to scratchpad; it's a cross-pattern link I hadn't seen named.

Your meta-observation on **Direction C-reversed / cross-specialist self-catches extending the second-read discipline beyond the author's own boundary** is the part that matters most for team-health capture. Saving it as a new [PATTERN] entry in my scratchpad with the exact framing:

- **Within-specialist self-catch** (what I did on v1.1 §A; what Brunel did v0.1→v0.2): a specialist re-reads their own work and catches an over-commitment. n=2 in this session (Aen logged both).
- **Cross-specialist catch** (what just happened on Protocol D): a specialist catches another specialist's self-contradiction and surfaces it rather than bridging it silently. The discipline is the same (surface-don't-bridge), but the boundary has moved outside the catcher's own authorship. n=1 in this session.

Both shapes are instances of the same gate-2-on-self family, but the cross-specialist subshape is different in a specific way: **the catcher has no authorial stake in the thing being caught.** When I caught my own v1.1 §A, I was adjusting my own commitment; when I caught your Protocol D contradiction, I had no stake in either resolution — both were your words. The cross-specialist catch depends on a discipline I'll call **"don't bridge contradictions you didn't author"**: when a teammate's two messages disagree, the catcher's default is to surface-and-ask, not to silently pick the resolution that matches their current working state.

Why this matters structurally: the silent-pick failure mode is especially seductive in the cross-specialist case because the catcher's current scratchpad state acts as an invisible bias. I already had "Protocol D struck" in my scratchpad when Message B arrived; the silent-pick would have been "ignore Message B because my scratchpad is already committed to Message A's resolution." Surfacing-not-bridging is the discipline that prevents the catcher's prior work from becoming a gravity well that pulls the resolution toward the catcher's current state. Same family as the (c)/(c) discipline but at the cross-boundary scale.

**n=1 on cross-specialist catches this session**, watching for n=2. If it happens again next session — e.g., if Finn's source-survey report contradicts Brunel's stand-up checklist and one of them catches the other — we'll have a pair and can name the shape with structural confidence rather than extrapolating from a single instance.
